question.text: A natural gas distribution pipeline develops a leak that releases 150 MCF of gas into a residential basement, causing evacuation of three families and estimated property damage of $12,000. Under 49 CFR 191, what are the reporting requirements?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 191.5, this is a distribution incident requiring evaluation for reporting. Immediate telephone reporting is NOT required for distribution per 191.5(a)(2)—immediate reporting applies only to transmission. Written reporting under 191.15 is required if any of the following apply: (1) property damage exceeding $50,000 (this incident has $12,000 - below threshold), (2) fire or explosion (not mentioned - unless gas ignited), (3) injury requiring hospitalization (not mentioned), (4) death (not mentioned), (5) evacuation of individuals not directly involved (three families evacuated - this applies if they were not responders, but the trigger requires both evacuation and other conditions). The incident likely does not meet mandatory written reporting threshold but should be evaluated for significance judgment. However, the operator must: investigate the leak per 192.617, make repairs following Part 192 standards, document all findings, evaluate why leak occurred, determine if similar condition exists elsewhere, and consider whether to voluntarily report as significant. The operator SHOULD report if: the leak indicates a systematic problem, similar leaks are occurring, the leak could have been worse, or the cause is unusual. State reporting requirements may have lower thresholds. PHM SA encourages reporting of events that don't meet thresholds but represent safety concerns. Under 192.703, if any person could be injured, the leak classification could be Grade 1 or 2. The operator must file a distribution incident report (Form PHMSA F 7100.2) within 30 days if reporting. Records must be maintained per 192.13.

question.text: A gas transmission pipeline operator has segments in Class 1, 2, and 3 locations. The operator uses infrared leak detection equipment for surveys. What are the maximum survey intervals under 49 CFR 192.706, and how does equipment type affect compliance?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.706(a), maximum leak survey intervals for transmission pipelines are: Class 1 - once every 5 calendar years, Class 2 - once every 3 calendar years, Class 3 - once per calendar year (annually). Infrared and optical gas imaging (OGI) equipment can be used if: under 192.706(b), the operator demonstrates that equipment can detect leaks with equal or greater sensitivity than conventional methods, sensitivity must be at least equivalent to detecting 200 ppm methane or better, the operator maintains records of equipment calibration and validation, OGI must be validated for the specific conditions including: temperature, humidity, wind, and background interference, OGI operators must be trained on interpreting thermal images, equipment must be maintained per manufacturer specifications, and records must demonstrate equal or superior effectiveness. Under 192.723(a), leak detection equipment must be appropriate for the environment. For surveys conducted with OGI: the operator must survey all accessible areas of the ROW, indirect readings may require confirmation with contact methods, barometric pressure and weather conditions may affect detection capability, documentation must show coverage was adequate, if equipment demonstrates superior performance (detects smaller leaks than conventional methods), it may provide enhanced safety but does not change interval requirements. The intervals are based on class location risk—higher risk equals more frequent surveys—regardless of equipment quality. Operators may survey more frequently based on risk or operator choice, but the regulation specifies maximum intervals. Survey records must be maintained per 192.13 including: dates, locations, equipment used, personnel, and any leaks found.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.616 and API RP 1162, what are the minimum message content requirements for pipeline public awareness communications?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.616 and API RP 1162, minimum message content requirements include: (1) PIPELINE PURPOSE AND RELIABILITY: information about the pipeline's function, importance to energy supply, and operator commitment to safety, (2) PRODUCT TRANSPORTED: identification of natural gas or other products, characteristics including odorization, and basic safety properties, (3) ONE-CALL NOTIFICATION: explanation of the legal requirement to call 811 before excavating, how the one-call system works, typical response times, and consequences of failing to call, (4) LEAK RECOGNITION: how to recognize a gas leak including: odor of mercaptan (rotten egg smell), hissing or roaring sounds, blowing dirt, bubbling water, discolored vegetation, and frozen ground, (5) LEAK RESPONSE: immediate actions to take including: leave the area immediately, avoid creating ignition sources (no phones, switches, vehicles), call 911 and the operator, and do not attempt repairs, (6) EMERGENCY CONTACT INFORMATION: operator 24/7 emergency numbers clearly displayed or provided, (7) PIPELINE LOCATION: general pipeline route information, marker locations, and access information, (8) SAFETY MESSAGING: emphasis on public safety and the importance of reporting unusual conditions, (9) EXCAVATOR-SPECIFIC: tolerance zone requirements, hand-digging mandates, and damage prevention responsibilities, (10) EMERGENCY OFFICIAL TRAINING: specialized information for first responders including valve locations, isolation procedures, and coordination protocols. Messages must be: appropriate for the audience (residents need different information than excavators), provided in languages appropriate for the community, clear and understandable (avoid excessive technical jargon), action-oriented (what to do), and repeated regularly through multiple channels. Effectiveness must be evaluated annually. Stakeholder feedback should inform message improvements.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.801, what is the definition of a 'covered task' for Operator Qualification purposes, and what criteria must operators use to identify covered tasks?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.801, a covered task is defined as 'an activity, identified by the operator, that affects the operation or integrity of the pipeline, is performed on a pipeline facility, and is performed in response to an abnormal operating condition requiring immediate action.' The three key criteria are: (1) AFFECTS OPERATION OR INTEGRITY—tasks that could impact pipeline safety, pressure integrity, structural integrity, or leak-tightness, including: welding, joining, inspecting, testing, installing, replacing, repairing, operating, or monitoring, (2) PERFORMED ON A PIPELINE FACILITY—tasks performed on pipelines, compressor stations, metering stations, valves, appurtenances, or other pipeline equipment, (3) PERFORMS IN RESPONSE TO ABNORMAL OPERATING CONDITION—tasks that may need to be performed when unusual conditions occur that require immediate attention. Operators must identify covered tasks using the process in 192.807: (1) systematically evaluate all activities performed on the pipeline, (2) assess whether improper performance could create a hazard (injury to public, employees, environment, property damage), (3) determine whether specialized knowledge or skill is required, (4) compare with industry standard task lists from organizations like PHMSA or INGAA, and (5) document each covered task with sufficient detail. Common covered tasks include: welding (various types), valve maintenance and operation, leak detection and repair, pressure testing, corrosion inspection and monitoring, regulator and relief valve work, pipeline inspection, and emergency response. Non-covered tasks typically include: administrative work, equipment operation that doesn't affect pipeline integrity, and supervisory oversight without physical work. Tasks are operator-specific—two operators may classify similar tasks differently based on their systems and procedures.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.631(a) and related requirements, what are the qualification and training requirements for control room personnel, and how do these differ from field personnel OQ requirements?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.631(a), control room personnel (controllers) must be qualified under the Operator Qualification program per 192 Subpart N before assuming control duties. Specific requirements: (1) controllers must be qualified on covered tasks including: abnormal operating condition response, emergency procedures, alarm response, SCADA system operation, and other control room activities identified by the operator, (2) initial qualification must include: written or practical examination, performance observation, or other approved evaluation method, (3) controllers must complete training on: the specific pipeline system controlled, SCADA alarms and setpoints, alarm management plan, abnormal operating procedures, emergency response coordination, communication protocols, and fatigue management, (4) requalification must occur at intervals not exceeding 3 years, (5) controllers must be able to demonstrate understanding of the limitations of their qualification. Differences from field OQ: (1) control room tasks are generally unique to each operator's system, making standardization more difficult, (2) CRM training includes fatigue management which is unique to control rooms, (3) controllers must be trained on alarm prioritization and management, (4) control room environments require additional human factors training, (5) CRM rule requires specific training on communication protocols, (6) controllers may need additional security clearance or background checks for control room access, (7) control room training often includes simulation of emergency scenarios. Similarities to field OQ: (1) 3-year re-evaluation requirement, (2) recordkeeping requirements per 192.811, (3) method of evaluation must be documented, (4) operators may accept third-party evaluations if equivalent. Both field and control room personnel must be qualified before performing covered tasks. The operator must have a process for evaluating and accepting previous experience or certifications. Controllers must understand when to escalate issues and when situations exceed their qualification.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.949, what must operators evaluate annually regarding their integrity management programs, and what actions are required if evaluation reveals program inadequacies?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.949, operators must conduct annual evaluations of their integrity management program effectiveness. Required evaluation elements include: (1) assessment of whether the program meets its stated objectives of enhancing pipeline safety by identifying and reducing integrity risks, (2) review of performance measures including: number of leaks eliminated or repaired, assessment completion rates, response times to identified threats, repair backlog statistics, threat identification accuracy, and comparison to industry benchmarks, (3) analysis of any incidents or near-misses that occurred since the last evaluation to determine if the IM program could have prevented them, (4) review of assessment results to determine if threats were accurately identified and if the program effectively addresses those threats, (5) examination of inspection and maintenance activities to ensure they are occurring as scheduled and are effective, (6) coordination with public safety officials to evaluate emergency response preparedness, (7) evaluation of new threats identified by industry experience or research and whether they apply to the operator's system, (8) review of data integration processes to ensure all available data is being considered, (9) analysis of cost-effectiveness and resource allocation, (10) assessment of whether assessment intervals are appropriate based on threat evaluation. If inadequacies are identified: (1) operators must develop an action plan with specific corrective actions, timelines, and responsible parties, (2) implement immediate corrective measures for serious deficiencies, (3) re-evaluate threat prioritization if current schedule is not addressing serious risks, (4) consider additional assessment methods or increased frequency for problem areas, (5) update program procedures and documentation, (6) provide additional training if personnel inadequacies identified, (7) document all findings and corrective actions, (8) establish metrics to verify corrective actions are effective, and (9) reassess within timeframe specified in the action plan. PHMSA may request the annual evaluation during inspections. Operators must maintain evaluation records for the life of the pipeline plus 5 years. Significant program changes may require PHMSA notification under management of change procedures.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 199.101 and 199.105, who is subject to DOT drug and alcohol testing requirements, and what constitutes a 'covered function' for pipeline operators?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 199.101 and 199.105, the following are subject to testing: (1) EMPLOYEES performing covered functions on a pipeline regulated under 49 CFR Parts 192, 193, or 195, (2) CONTRACTORS and their employees if performing covered functions for or on behalf of the operator, (3) TEMPORARY workers, part-time workers, and leased workers performing covered functions. Covered functions include any duties related to: (1) OPERATION OF PIPELINE FACILITIES - activities that could affect the safety of pipeline operation including: controlling pressure, flow, and routing of gas, operating compressor or pumping equipment, monitoring SCADA systems, responding to alarms and abnormal conditions, (2) MAINTENANCE OF PIPELINE FACILITIES - performing work on pipelines, valves, regulators, meters, compressor stations, and other equipment including: welding, joining, cutting, tapping, valve maintenance, regulator maintenance, and leak repair, (3) EMERGENCY RESPONSE - responding to leaks, ruptures, or other emergencies, operating emergency equipment, and coordinating responses, and (4) other functions identified by the operator as affecting safety. The term 'covered function' is broader than 'covered task' under OQ—drug testing applies to anyone whose performance affects the safety of pipeline operations, while OQ applies only to specific tasks. Key differences: (1) drug testing applies to functions affecting safety, not just specific tasks, (2) drug testing rates apply to the entire workforce performing covered functions (50% random for drugs, 10% for alcohol), (3) drug testing is not task-specific, (4) contractors are subject to the same requirements as direct employees, (5) testing must occur before performing covered functions (pre-employment). Persons NOT subject to testing include: administrative staff with no safety function, purely clerical positions, and personnel whose work products do not affect physical operations. The operator must identify covered functions in their written program and ensure all affected personnel are in the testing pool.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.619(a)(2), under what circumstances must an operator establish MAOP by reducing operating pressure, and what is the minimum required pressure reduction?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.619(a)(2), an operator must operate at a reduced pressure if actual operating history does not demonstrate ability to operate safely at current MAOP. Circumstances requiring pressure reduction: (1) the highest pressure during the preceding 5 years was lower than the current MAOP, (2) pressure control devices have limited the operating pressure below the designated MAOP, (3) the pipeline cannot be tested to 1.25× current MAOP due to condition, (4) anomalies found during integrity assessment indicate reduced pressure rating, (5) class location change requires lower design pressure, (6) component limitations (valves, flanges) cannot support current pressure, (7) cathodic protection issues create integrity concerns. The minimum pressure reduction requirement: under 192.619(a)(2), MAOP must be established at a pressure not exceeding the highest pressure sustained for the required period (typically 5 years continuous or 5 of 8 years). There is no specific percentage reduction required—the reduction must be sufficient to match demonstrated performance capability. However: if actual operating pressure has been limited to a significantly lower level (e.g., 20% below current MAOP), the operator should establish MAOP at or below that demonstrated level, the goal is to operate at a pressure that can be sustained based on actual experience or testing, and the operator should consider a safety margin below the demonstrated pressure. If the operator needs to increase pressure above the demonstrated level: hydrostatic testing to 1.25× the desired MAOP is required per 192.505. Pressure reduction must be: documented with supporting data, reviewed considering system operating requirements, implemented gradually if possible to avoid operational issues, communicated to affected customers if capacity changes, evaluated for impact on pipeline integrity, and coordinated with component pressure ratings. Records must be maintained showing basis for reduction. If pressure was reduced due to integrity concerns, reassessment may be required before returning to higher pressure.

scoring_guide.good: 75-89: Core circumstances, reduction basis, implementation
scoring_guide.fair: 60-74: Basic concept but misses key elements
scoring_guide.poor: Below 60: Fundamental errors

question.text: What are the requirements under 49 CFR 192.907 for treating combined HCAs where a single pipeline segment meets criteria for both Type 1 (identified site) and Type 2 (urbanized area) classifications?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.903 and 192.907, when a pipeline segment simultaneously meets criteria for both Type 1 (identified site) and Type 2 (urbanized area) HCAs: (1) the entire area meeting either criterion is treated as an HCA requiring integrity management program coverage, (2) the operator can elect to treat the combined area as a single HCA segment for assessment and scheduling purposes, simplifying program administration, (3) alternatively, the operator may segment the area into discrete portions based on the specific trigger type—useful when different threats predominate or when assessment methods differ in applicability, (4) the potential impact radius (PIR) calculation method under 192.905 applies regardless of HCA type, (5) baseline assessment requirements apply to the entire HCA area without distinction between Type 1 and Type 2 portions, (6) the operator may use varying assessment methods: Type 1 areas (specific sites like schools) may prioritize ILI or hydrostatic testing to ensure site-specific integrity, Type 2 areas (urbanized zones) may utilize direct assessment or pressure testing where ILI access is limited by tight spaces. Important considerations: document the basis for combining or segmenting HCAs, ensure all HCA mileage is captured in the integrity management program, apply the most stringent requirements when there is any overlap or ambiguity, notify PHMSA of the approach if questioned during inspections, and evaluate whether combined treatment affects threat prioritization—Type 2 areas may have different threat profiles (excavation damage, third-party contact) than Type 1 areas. Combined HCAs do not change: reassessment intervals (based on assessment method, not HCA type), performance measure requirements, or annual evaluation obligations. The operator should: maintain maps clearly delineating HCA boundaries, track HCA mileage by type for reporting purposes, and review combined HCAs during the 5-year HCA verification cycle.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.745, what inspection requirements apply to sectionalizing block valves and other valves on transmission lines, and what constitutes 'partially operating' a valve during inspection?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.745(b), valves on transmission lines must be inspected and partially operated at least every 7 months. However, valves identified as low risk per 192.745(b)(1) may be inspected at 12-month intervals if the operator documents why longer intervals are appropriate. Valve inspection requirements: (1) INSPECTION: visual examination of the valve box or enclosure, checking for deterioration, corrosion, or damage, verifying the valve is properly marked and accessible, ensuring security features are intact, checking for evidence of leakage, and verifying the valve turns freely, (2) PARTIAL OPERATION: this means cycling the valve through a portion of its travel (typically 1/4 to 1/2 of full rotation) and returning it to the original open or closed position, different from full operation (complete close and reopen) which is typically done only during major maintenance, demonstrates the valve is not seized and actuator mechanisms are functional, reveals any binding or mechanical issues, and confirms the valve can be operated in an emergency, (3) INSPECTION DOCUMENTATION: date of inspection, valve identification and location, current valve position, results of visual inspection, results of partial operation, any abnormalities found and actions taken, and name of inspector, (4) CORRECTIVE ACTION: valves cannot be fully closed must be repaired or replaced within specified timeframes, valves that cannot be partially operated must be addressed immediately, and security deficiencies must be remedied promptly, and (5) EXCLUDED VALVES: this requirement applies to block valves and other valves required for safety or operation, check valves are generally excluded from exercise requirements unless specific inspection programs apply, and relief valves have separate testing requirements. Under 192.745(a), valve requirements apply to valves that might be required during an emergency. Automated and remote-controlled valves (192.745(d)) have additional testing requirements for automation systems. Valve records must be maintained for life of the pipeline plus 5 years. Valve inspection should be scheduled to avoid system disruptions—operators typically rotate through valves rather than inspecting all simultaneously.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.614 and related regulations, what must operators do when excavation damage occurs to their pipeline, and what reporting requirements apply?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.614 and related regulations, when excavation damage occurs: (1) IMMEDIATE RESPONSE: assess situation for emergency conditions (leaks, pressure loss, structural damage), take immediate action to protect public safety including evacuation if necessary, isolate damaged section by closing valves if indicated, control ignition sources, and call appropriate emergency responders, (2) INVESTIGATION: document the incident scene including photographs, interview excavator and witnesses, review one-call records to verify locate request was made, locate marks, and operator response, determine if operator marks were correct, evaluate if excavator followed tolerance zone requirements, assess the extent of damage to pipe integrity, and identify root cause, (3) DAMAGE ASSESSMENT: under 192.613, evaluate whether the pipeline can remain in service, determine if immediate repair is required, apply appropriate repair methods per Subpart K, and consider whether excavation indicates a risk of additional similar damage in the area, (4) REPAIR: follow repair standards in 192.933 for HCA lines, repair or replace damaged section, and pressure test if required by damage severity, (5) REPORTING: transmission line damage meeting criteria must be reported per 49 CFR 191 if thresholds met (death, injury, $50K+ damage, 3 MCF+ gas loss); distribution operators must report damage statistics annually per 192.1005 including number of damages by cause, (6) COST RECOVERY: pursue recovery of repair costs from responsible party under state damage prevention laws, document all costs associated with repair, and maintain records for legal proceedings, (7) FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS: enhance marking or monitoring in the area if needed, retrain excavators if procedures were not followed, evaluate locate accuracy and response time, and report serious or repeated violations to one-call enforcement authorities. For significant damage: incident report required per 191.5 for transmission; PHMSA may investigate; state regulatory notification may be required; and operators should improve public awareness and damage prevention efforts in the area.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.5(a)(4), what distinguishes a Class 4 location from a Class 3 location, and what special considerations apply to establishing Class 4 boundaries?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.5(a)(4), a Class 4 location is any class location unit where buildings with four or more stories above ground are prevalent. Key distinctions from Class 3: (1) CLASS 4 THRESHOLD: buildings must be at least 4 stories above ground—not just tall buildings, (2) PREVALENT STANDARD: the term 'prevalent' generally interpreted as multiple 4+ story buildings in an area, not just isolated structures, (3) CLASS 4 VS CLASS 3: Class 3 has high density but buildings may be low-rise (46+ buildings but less than 4 stories), Class 4 has multi-story high-rise development, Class 3 uses horizontal distance (220 yards), Class 4 is based on vertical building height. Special considerations for Class 4 boundaries: (1) VALVE SPACING: Class 4 requires the shortest spacing—2.5 miles maximum, (2) MAOP LIMITATIONS: design factor is lowest (0.40), significantly limiting pressure, (3) HYDROSTATIC TESTING: existing pipelines may have difficulty testing to 1.25× MAOP for Class 4, requiring careful assessment of pipe condition, (4) EGRESS CONCERNS: high-rise areas require special emergency planning for evacuation of tall structures, (5) PUBLIC AWARENESS: targeted communications to high-rise building management and residents who may not be aware of surface-level pipeline, (6) INCIDENT CONSEQUENCES: HIR (hazard identification radius) and PIR calculations may be insufficient for high-rise areas, requiring enhanced modeling, (7) INTEGRITY MANAGEMENT: high-rise areas automatically become HCAs due to population density. Class 4 determination: usually occurs in major urban centers, requires careful assessment of building heights, not just counts, boundaries extend the CLU length (1 mile) once 4+ story criterion met. Under 192.611, when an area becomes Class 4: notification to PHMSA required per 192.13, MAOP reduction or physical upgrading required, and very short implementation timeline due to high population at risk. Operators should: maintain current aerial photography or databases of building heights, evaluate Class 4 potential when new high-rise construction is planned, and consider proactive pressure reduction or relocation before Class 4 designation.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.616 and API RP 1162, what documentation must operators maintain for their public awareness programs, and what minimum retention periods apply?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.616 and API RP 1162, operators must maintain documentation of public awareness activities including: (1) WRITTEN PROGRAM DOCUMENT: current version of the public awareness program document including objectives, audiences, methods, and schedules, (2) DELIVERY RECORDS: for each communication sent or delivered, documentation must include: date of communication, method used (mail, meeting, electronic), audience or recipient group, specific content or materials distributed, and person responsible for delivery, (3) MEETING RECORDS: for public meetings or stakeholder sessions: date and location, attendees (including sign-in sheets or estimated count), topics covered, presentation materials, and feedback received, (4) EVALUATION RECORDS: annual evaluations including evaluation methods used, results and findings, changes implemented based on evaluation, and comparison to performance metrics, (5) EMERGENCY OFFICIAL COORDINATION: records of communications with emergency response agencies including training provided and joint exercises, (6) EXCAVATOR OUTREACH: documentation of one-call center participation, communications with excavators, and any damage prevention education provided, (7) COMPLAINTS AND INQUIRIES: log of public inquiries or complaints and responses provided, (8) CORRECTIVE ACTIONS: documentation of improvements made to the program. RETENTION PERIOD: under API RP 1162, records must be retained for at least 3 years; however, some operators retain longer for legal or trend analysis purposes; PHMSA Form F 7100.2 requires reporting of public awareness program summary information annually. Under 192.616(c), operators must evaluate program effectiveness annually and document findings. question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.706, what procedures must operators follow if they cannot complete a required leak survey within the specified timeframe, such as due to access restrictions or weather conditions?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.706, if an operator cannot complete a required leak survey within the specified timeframe (5 years for Class 1, 3 years for Class 2, 1 year for Class 3/4 for transmission lines): (1) INTERIM MEASURES: implement compensatory measures including increased patrol frequency, temporary pressure reduction, enhanced one-call monitoring, or other risk mitigation until survey can be completed, (2) PHMSA NOTIFICATION: per 192.937(b) for integrity management, if an operator cannot complete reassessment within required intervals, they must notify PHMSA and explain reasons; for leak surveys specifically, notification is prudent if delays are significant, (3) DOCUMENTATION: maintain records explaining why survey was delayed, what interim measures were implemented, and when survey will be completed, (4) ACCESS ISSUES: for right-of-way access restrictions—work with landowners to obtain access, consider legal options if access denied, or assess if alternative survey methods (aerial, alternate route) can provide equivalent coverage, (5) WEATHER DELAYS: for extreme weather—document weather conditions preventing survey, reschedule for appropriate season, and ensure cumulative delays don't exceed regulatory maximums, (6) PRIORITIZATION: if surveys are delayed in multiple areas, prioritize highest risk locations (highest class, near HCAs, older pipe, leak history), (7) EQUIPMENT ISSUES: if survey equipment is unavailable—expedite repair/replacement or contract with alternative providers, (8) CONTRACTOR DELAYS: if using third-party surveyors—hold contractors accountable or seek alternative providers, (9) RESCHEDULING: commit to specific completion date and track progress, and (10) FOLLOW-UP: complete survey as soon as practical and document any anomalies found. The regulations do not provide a grace period—operators must make every effort to complete surveys on time. Significant delays may be considered non-compliance. Operators should build surveying capacity, maintain backup equipment and contractors, and plan surveys early in the interval to avoid deadline pressure. Chronic delays require program correction.

question.text: Under 49 CFR 192.633, what are the requirements for written procedures addressing SCADA system security, and how must operators protect control systems from unauthorized access or cyber threats?

questions.expected_response_detailed: Under 49 CFR 192.633 and supporting guidance, operators must implement measures to protect control systems including: (1) ACCESS CONTROLS: physical security for control room including card access, visitor logs, and limited entry points; logical security including user authentication, password policies, and role-based access, (2) NETWORK SEGMENTATION: isolation of SCADA networks from corporate networks and internet; use of firewalls and intrusion detection systems; demilitarized zones (DMZ) for external connections, (3) SYSTEM MONITORING: continuous monitoring for unusual activity; logging of access attempts and system changes; regular review of security logs, (4) PATCH MANAGEMENT: procedures for evaluating and applying security patches; testing patches before deployment; managing legacy systems that cannot be patched, (5) MALWARE PROTECTION: antivirus and anti-malware software on applicable systems; regular updates; email filtering at control room boundaries, (6) BACKUP SYSTEMS: redundant SCADA systems or manual backup procedures; backup power for critical systems; data backups to ensure recovery capability, (7) INCIDENT RESPONSE: procedures for responding to cyber incidents; communication protocols during security events; coordination with IT security and management, (8) VENDOR MANAGEMENT: security requirements for vendors accessing systems; supervision of vendor activities; change control for vendor modifications, (9) EMPLOYEE TRAINING: security awareness training for controllers and staff; social engineering awareness; clean desk policies in control rooms, and (10) CHANGE CONTROL: management of change procedures for SCADA system modifications; testing and approval requirements; rollback procedures. Specific requirements: maintain written security procedures as part of the CRM program, review and update procedures annually or when threats change, test security measures periodically, and coordinate with PHMSA during significant incidents. TSA pipeline security directives impose additional requirements including security assessments and reporting. The Security and Exchange Commission (SEC) requires disclosure of material cyber incidents for public companies. Operators should align pipeline security with broader corporate cybersecurity programs.

scoring_guide.excellent: 90-100: Physical and logical access controls, network segmentation/isolation, backup systems, incident response, training, annual review
scoring_guide.good: 75-89: Core security elements, most controls
scoring_guide.fair: 60-74: Basic security concept but gaps
scoring_guide.poor: Below 60: Fundamental errors

abnormal_variants: Suspected intrusion detected - incident response activated
Legacy system cannot receive patches - compensatory controls required
Vendor access without authorization - security review and policy update
